parts.content: Calculate the mitotic index for both normal lung tissue and lung tumor tissue. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Uses correct formula: $\text{Mitotic index} = \frac{\text{cells in mitosis}}{\text{total cells}} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct calculation and answer for normal lung tissue: $\frac{25}{500}\times 100 = 5\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct calculation and answer for lung tumor tissue: $\frac{120}{500}\times 100 = 24\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Award ECF for one correct mitotic index if the other contains an arithmetic error but correct method is shown <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why a higher mitotic index is observed in cancerous tissue compared to normal tissue.
parts.markscheme:
- Cancer cells undergo more frequent cell division due to loss of normal cell-cycle control (e.g. mutations affecting checkpoint regulation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore a greater proportion of cells are in mitosis at any one time, increasing the mitotic index <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Cancer cells divide uncontrollably/continuously compared with normal cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This leads to more cells being observed in mitosis in a given sample, giving a higher mitotic index <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: Calculates $\frac{\text{cells in mitosis}}{\text{cells in interphase}}=\frac{40}{160}=0.25$; mitotic index should be $\frac{\text{cells in mitosis}}{\text{total cells}}$.
Calculates $\frac{\text{cells in interphase}}{\text{total cells}}=\frac{160}{200}=0.80$; mitotic index uses mitotic cells, not interphase cells.
Cells in mitosis = $16+10+4+10=40$. Total cells = $160+40=200$. Mitotic index $=\frac{40}{200}=0.20$.
Calculates $\frac{\text{anaphase cells}}{\text{total cells}}=\frac{4}{200}=0.02$ (or similar); mitotic index includes all mitotic stages (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase).

parts.content: Outline the process of mitosis.
parts.markscheme:
- Prophase: chromosomes condense and spindle fibres form <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Metaphase: chromosomes align at the cell equator <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Anaphase: sister chromatids separate and move to poles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Telophase: chromosomes decondense and nuclear envelopes reform <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Results in two genetically identical nuclei <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains chromosome number <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cytokinesis often follows to complete cell division <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between mitosis and meiosis.
parts.markscheme:
- Mitosis produces diploid cells; meiosis produces haploid cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitosis involves one division; meiosis involves two divisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitosis produces genetically identical cells; meiosis produces genetically varied cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitosis occurs in somatic cells; meiosis occurs in gamete-producing cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitosis does not involve crossing over; meiosis does <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mitosis used for growth/repair; meiosis for reproduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Homologous chromosomes do not pair in mitosis <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the cell cycle is controlled to prevent uncontrolled cell division.
parts.markscheme:
- Cell cycle has regulatory checkpoints (G1, G2, M) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- G1 checks for DNA damage before replication <marks>1 mark</marks>
- G2 ensures DNA replication is complete <marks>1 mark</marks>
- M checkpoint ensures chromosomes are properly aligned <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cyclins bind to CDKs to regulate progression <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tumour suppressor genes (e.g. p53) halt cycle if errors detected <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proto-oncogenes promote normal division; mutations lead to oncogenes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Failure in regulation can lead to cancer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Apoptosis may be triggered if repair fails <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of cyclins in controlling the cell cycle.
parts.markscheme:
Cyclins are proteins that regulate the progression of the cell cycle by activating cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs). <marks>1 mark</marks>

They ensure that the cell cycle progresses in the correct sequence and at the appropriate times. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Different cyclins are active at different stages of the cell cycle, and their levels fluctuate in a cyclical manner. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Cyclin and CDK Interaction
steps.content:
Cyclins are a group of proteins that control the progression of the cell cycle. They do not have enzymatic activity themselves; instead, they function by binding to enzymes called **Cyclin-Dependent Kinases (CDKs)**. 

This physical binding is the essential first step in "turning on" the machinery required for cell division.

steps.title: Activation of Enzyme Complexes
steps.content:
When a cyclin binds to a CDK, they form a **cyclin-CDK complex**. This complex acts as an active enzyme. 

Once activated, the CDK performs its biochemical role by phosphorylating (attaching phosphate groups to) specific target proteins. This chemical modification triggers the specific cellular activities required for a particular phase, such as DNA replication in the $S$ phase or nuclear envelope breakdown in Mitosis.

highlights.text: regulate the progression of the cell cycle

steps.title: Fluctuation and Sequential Control
steps.content:
To ensure the cell cycle moves in the right order, different cyclins reach peak concentrations at different times. 

1. **Cyclical Levels:** Cyclin levels rise and fall in a specific pattern.
2. **Thresholds:** A specific phase only begins when its corresponding cyclin reaches a high enough concentration to activate enough CDKs.
3. **Progression:** This biochemical "timer" ensures that the cell cycle progresses in the correct sequence and only when the cell is ready.

highlights.text: ensure that the cell cycle progresses in the correct sequence and at the appropriate times
different cyclins are active at different stages of the cell cycle, and their levels fluctuate in a cyclical manner

steps.title: Define Cyclins and CDKs
steps.content:
To understand how the cell cycle is controlled, we first need to identify the "players." Cyclins are a group of regulatory proteins. They work by binding to enzymes called **Cyclin-Dependent Kinases** ($CDKs$). 

When a specific cyclin binds to its partner $CDK$, the enzyme becomes active. This active complex then adds phosphate groups to other proteins, which "switches on" the biological processes required for a specific stage of the cell cycle (like DNA replication or mitosis).

highlights.text: Control of the cell cycle using cyclins (HL)

steps.title: Focus on Temporal Fluctuations
steps.content:
The "control" part comes from the fact that cyclin levels are not constant; they fluctuate in a predictable, cyclical pattern. 

Each type of cyclin (e.g., Cyclin D, E, A, and B) has a specific time during the cycle when its concentration increases. For example, a specific cyclin's concentration will rise rapidly just before the cell needs to move into the $S$ phase (DNA replication) and then drop off once that stage is complete. This **temporal (time-based) change** ensures that the cell only moves forward when the concentration reaches a specific threshold.

highlights.text: levels fluctuate in a cyclical manner

steps.title: Ensure Correct Sequential Progression
steps.content:
Because different cyclins peak at different times, they act as a chronological "checklist." This ensures the **sequential order** of the cell cycle: 

1. A stage only begins when its specific cyclin reaches a high enough concentration to activate the necessary $CDKs$.
2. The next stage cannot begin until the previous stage's cyclins are degraded.

This system prevents the cell from performing tasks out of order—for example, it ensures the cell doesn't try to divide its nucleus (mitosis) before it has finished copying its $DNA$ ($S$ phase).

highlights.text: progresses in the correct sequence and at the appropriate times

parts.content: Outline one event occurring at each of the G1, G2, and M checkpoints.
parts.markscheme:
### G1 checkpoint
- The cell checks for DNA damage 

OR 

- The cell ensures that it is of adequate size and has sufficient nutrients <marks>1 mark</marks>

### G2 checkpoint
- The cell verifies that DNA replication in S phase has been completed successfully 

OR
- The cell checks for DNA damage <marks>1 mark</marks>

### M checkpoint
- The cell ensures that all chromosomes are properly attached to the spindle apparatus before proceeding with mitosis <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand the cell cycle checkpoints
steps.content: In **Topic D2.1: Cell and nuclear division**, we learn that the cell cycle is a highly regulated process. Checkpoints act as control points where the cell "decides" whether to proceed to the next phase or pause to fix errors. To answer this question, we simply need to recall the specific regulatory criteria for the G1, G2, and M phases.

steps.title: Outline the G1 checkpoint
steps.content:
The **G1 checkpoint** occurs at the end of the G1 phase, just before the cell commits to DNA replication in the S phase. 

At this stage, the cell monitors its environment and its own health. A key event is checking for **DNA damage**. If the DNA is intact and the cell has reached an **adequate size** with sufficient nutrients, it receives the signal to proceed to the S phase.

highlights.text: The cell checks for DNA damage
The cell ensures that it is of adequate size and has sufficient nutrients

steps.title: Outline the G2 checkpoint
steps.content:
The **G2 checkpoint** occurs after DNA replication is finished but before mitosis begins. 

The primary goal here is to ensure the safety of the genetic material before it is divided. The cell verifies that **DNA replication** was completed successfully and again checks for any **DNA damage** that may have occurred during the S phase. This prevents mutations from being passed on to daughter cells.

highlights.text: The cell verifies that DNA replication in S phase has been completed successfully
The cell checks for DNA damage

steps.title: Outline the M checkpoint
steps.content:
The **M checkpoint** (also known as the spindle checkpoint) occurs during metaphase of mitosis. 

Before the cell allows the sister chromatids to separate during anaphase, it must ensure that every single **chromosome is properly attached** to the spindle fibers. This ensures that each daughter cell will receive the correct number of chromosomes, preventing conditions like Down syndrome (non-disjunction).

highlights.text: The cell ensures that all chromosomes are properly attached to the spindle apparatus before proceeding with mitosis

parts.content: Explain what is meant by water potential.
parts.markscheme:
- Water potential ($\Psi$) is the potential energy/tendency of water to move, relative to pure water. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pure water has $\Psi = 0$ (at standard conditions); solutions have a more negative $\Psi$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water moves by osmosis from higher (less negative) $\Psi$ to lower (more negative) $\Psi$ across a partially permeable membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water potential is the sum of solute potential and pressure potential: $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how osmosis affects plant cells placed in pure water and in salt solution.
parts.markscheme:
- Osmosis is the net movement of water across a partially permeable membrane from higher (less negative) water potential to lower (more negative) water potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In pure water, the external water potential is higher than inside the cell, so water enters the cell by osmosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The vacuole swells and the cytoplasm pushes the plasma membrane against the cell wall, increasing turgor/pressure potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The cell becomes turgid; the cell wall prevents bursting and limits further water uptake. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In salt solution, the external water potential is lower (more negative) than inside the cell, so water leaves the cell by osmosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The vacuole shrinks and the plasma membrane pulls away from the cell wall (plasmolysis); the cell becomes flaccid. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water loss reduces turgor/pressure potential; if the solution is sufficiently concentrated, plasmolysis may be irreversible and the cell may die. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the process of natural selection leading to the evolution of antibiotic resistance in a population of bacteria.
parts.markscheme:
- Genetic variation exists within the bacterial population due to random mutations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some mutations confer resistance to a specific antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The application of the antibiotic acts as a selective pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Susceptible bacteria are killed, while resistant individuals survive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resistant bacteria reproduce / undergo binary fission <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resistance alleles are passed on to the next generation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The frequency of the resistant phenotype increases within the population over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 4 marks*</note>

parts.content: Explain the mechanisms of allopatric and sympatric speciation and how they lead to the divergence of species.
parts.markscheme:
- Speciation is the process by which one species splits into two or more distinct species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are geographically isolated by physical barriers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples include mountain ranges, oceans, or deserts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Geographic isolation prevents gene flow between the two populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each population is subject to different selective pressures / environmental conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympatric speciation occurs within the same geographical area / without physical separation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Behavioral isolation can occur if mating rituals or courtship songs change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temporal isolation occurs if populations begin breeding at different times / seasons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ecological isolation occurs if populations occupy different niches in the same habitat <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetic drift can cause further divergence, especially in small populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Divergence eventually leads to the inability of the groups to produce fertile offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 7 marks*</note>

parts.content: Using the pentadactyl limb as an example, explain how homologous structures provide evidence for evolution.
parts.markscheme:
- Homologous structures are anatomical features that share a similar basic structure but perform different functions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The pentadactyl limb consists of a specific arrangement of bones (humerus, radius/ulna, carpals, and five digits) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This shared structure across mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians suggests a common ancestor <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Differences in the limbs are adaptations for different environments / modes of life (e.g., flight, swimming, or running) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This pattern of diversification from a common structure is known as adaptive radiation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The greater the similarity in structure, the more closely related the species are likely to be <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 4 marks*</note>

parts.content: Using Figure 1 and the table, explain why some embryos end up with only $1\text{--}2$ aneuploid cells while others show $3$ or more abnormal cells.
parts.markscheme:
- Nondisjunction/segregation error early in cleavage (e.g., first/second mitosis) affects a larger proportion of descendant cells, so many cells become aneuploid (e.g., $3+$ abnormal cells) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Nondisjunction/segregation error later in cleavage affects fewer descendant cells, so only $1\text{--}2$ cells are aneuploid <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allow reference to unequal distribution during mitosis leading to different lineages (aneuploid vs euploid) in a mosaic embryo <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allow reference to lagging chromosomes/micronuclei causing loss of a chromosome in only one blastomere, limiting the number of abnormal cells <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate how many embryos in this study were fully abnormal (full aneuploidy) and explain why such embryos have a low chance of developing into live births.

- Accept: euploid cells may outcompete/replace aneuploid lineages during subsequent divisions, improving viability <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the phases of mitosis, outlining the main events that occur in prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase.
parts.markscheme:
**Prophase**

- Chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Nuclear envelope begins to disintegrate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spindle fibres start to form from the centrosomes <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Metaphase**

- Chromosomes align at the cell's equatorial plate <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spindle fibres attach to the centromeres of the chromosomes <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Anaphase**

- Sister chromatids are pulled apart by the spindle fibres towards opposite poles of the cell <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Telophase**

- Chromatids arrive at the poles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Chromosomes begin to de-condense back into chromatin <marks>1 mark</marks>

7 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each point up to a maximum of [7].</note>
